title: Carneddau (Black Ladders) Wellington Memorial
narrative: Wellington DV800 took off from RAF Lichfield on 19 July 1942 for a daytime cross-country navigation exercise. One leg of the planned route took the aircraft from Holyhead to Rhyl, running along the North Wales coast. The Court of Inquiry concluded that a navigation error, possibly compounded by wireless equipment failure, caused the crew to fly up the Ogwen Valley from Bethesda in cloud rather than following the coast. The aircraft struck the ridge between Carnedd Dafydd and Carnedd Llewelyn at about 2,500 feet in an area known as Black Ladders (Ysgolion Duon) and burned fiercely on impact. It was ten days before the crash site was located. A recovery team from RAF Llandwrog was dispatched to retrieve the crew. The two Australian airmen -- Longbottom from Western Australia and Bannister -- were buried together at Caernarfon (Llanbeblig) Cemetery. The crash site remains a designated war grave, and because bodies of two crewmen (F/O Evans and Sgt Gash -- NOTE: these are from a different crash, Lancaster NE132) were never fully recovered from the mountain, removal of any material is prohibited.
cause_of_loss: Navigation error in cloud; aircraft flew up valley from Bethesda into the mountainside at ~2,500 ft. Possible wireless equipment failure (last radio contact at 12:06)
memorial_details: The crash site on Black Ladders is itself treated as a protected war grave. Wreckage remains scattered on the mountainside. While there is no formal dedicated memorial plaque at this specific site, the area is widely recognised and documented by the RAF Mountain Rescue heritage community. The site is very remote, requiring experienced mountain walking to access.

external_narratives.summary: Detailed crash site analysis of Wellington Mk.IC DV800 of 27 OTU. The aircraft crashed on Bwlch Cyfryw-drum in the Carneddau on 19 July 1942 during a cross-country exercise from RAF Lichfield. The pilot descended through cloud to get a visual fix but the aircraft had strayed 10 miles off course and struck the ridge at approximately 2,500 feet.
key_details: Pilot descended through cloud for visual fix
Crash site not found for 10 days

external_narratives.summary: Aircrash Sites UK documents the Wellington DV800 crash site on Black Ladders in the Carneddau, noting that wreckage remains scattered on the mountainside. The site is treated as a protected war grave and removal of material is prohibited.
key_details: Remote location requiring experienced mountain walking
